body:
This command seems to give the approx delay. 

`starship explain`

Not sure if takes git into account.

---

You can try disabling the git configurations one by one and see if there is any difference in between. That could help tell if the problem are with starship or not.

--- 

While not a solution, it could help https://www.git-tower.com/blog/git-performance/
Yes, disabling git status and git branch makes everything faster.

What about one or the other?

---

https://www.git-tower.com/blog/git-performance/#how-to-speed-up-the-working-tree

There are multiple things that could the cause slow down on big projects, on your end what you can do, is look them up and optimize your git config. As It should benefit your environment. 

Many branches and commits can slow down those information retrievals.

Nonetheless, there could be issues with starship.

Ok, so I tried to check speed of fish loading prompt with hyperfine. 
But tbh data from there is not valid, I will share that as statistics:
Benchmark 1: fish --no-config -c exit
  Time (mean ± σ):     107.9 ms ± 274.4 ms    [User: 9.8 ms, System: 5.5 ms]
  Range (min … max):    15.8 ms … 888.7 ms    10 runs

Benchmark 2: fish -c exit
  Time (mean ± σ):     137.4 ms ±  11.1 ms    [User: 98.1 ms, System: 44.9 ms]
  Range (min … max):   131.2 ms … 175.8 ms    15 runs

That's made up numbers. Here is why:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c3bf4273-be00-42c8-ae98-af5ba7d0ac54
Imo loading time for that is not 131ms avg.

I fixed my config, sure, some progress there, but I think small repo vs large repo shouldn't make a big difference for loading something like branch name and branch icon.
Try to confirm if the problem happens only with starship git branch or status or both at the same time.

Starship can show module timings via `starship timings`. To benchmark the whole prompt execution, please try to benchmark `starship prompt` or `starship module module_name` for individual modules (this may not work if the module is disabled). The `git_branch` shouldn't execute the git-cli.

So here are timings (with default git status icons):
Here are the timings of modules in your prompt (>=1ms or output):
 git_status  -  61ms  -   "[!] "
 python      -  37ms  -   " v3.13.1"
 git_branch  -   3ms  -   " update-models"
 directory   -   2ms  -   "Content-Generation-Framework "
 time        -   1ms  -   " 10:21 "

Here are with my custom icons:
Here are the timings of modules in your prompt (>=1ms or output):
 git_status  -  78ms  -   " 🛠️"
 python      -  39ms  -   " v3.13.1"
 git_branch  -   8ms  -   " update-models"
 directory   -   3ms  -   "Content-Generation-Framework "
 time        -   2ms  -   " 10:21 "
(without git status remove 61/78 ms)

So tbh now i dunno what causes this behavior. I don't use git status no more. Still sometimes I have problem with ultra long loading (2-3 seconds). I guess that's state for now.

Maybe running the command after it has loaded, wont get the actual timings?

Try to set these to get timings while loading.

https://starship.rs/faq/#starship-is-doing-something-unexpected-how-can-i-debug-it

Does it happen after a fresh warp opening or after, when navigating folders?

Does it happen with any other terminal?

Yes, this issue occurs in other terminals as well. The only fix is to switch to a different shell or remove Starship.
The timing values remain the same as mentioned above, even after following all the steps in the tutorial.
Edit: It is happening when entering the big repo and cd'ing into other dirs in big repo, when I get out from big repo next commands will work fast as hell.

Different shell but with starship?

---

https://github.com/fish-shell/fish-shell/issues/8979

So same shit basically:

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0105c565-b44b-456e-a45b-ea3d8caabd43


https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ff29d431-e62a-4bbd-aedd-129d2a575723

You can try those fish git settings to see if help, as well as check your machine resource usage when that happens.

---

You could also try Nushell with Starship. It's another shell written in rust, very fast (it's the one that I am currently using). 

Possibly with a terminal like Alacritty, Wezterm, all are written in rust.

To check different combinations. I am saying this because I tried Warp before, not the most fluid.

https://alacritty.org/

https://www.nushell.sh/

https://wezterm.org/

Any potential bottleneck that you remove, it's more fluidity added.

---

Edit:

I previously recommended kitty for it's performance over the other terminals, but I removed. https://www.reddit.com/r/suckless/comments/to61qy/dont_use_kitty_terminal/

Another possible reason, and it could be the same issue you experience @MattOrmianek 

https://github.com/JanDeDobbeleer/oh-my-posh/discussions/1293#discussioncomment-11552949

---

I suppose starship could use that comment and improve their implementation.

---

There is already a request https://github.com/starship/starship/issues/5976

---

Though that seems to be for Windows. If so, maybe the problem in linux/macos are caused by something else.
Seems like same problem to me, hope it will be fixed. Thanks for updating.

Issue just happened today on a relatively small project.

After it happened (on the cd to the folder), there were some weird small hangs in each non git command typed afterwards. But it seemed to have stopped after I typed `git status`. So, there is definitely some bug in there.

Accordingly to this https://github.com/starship/starship/issues/5985 it's that starship stops working in attempt to give the snappier experience if the timeout are set low. 

Weird behavior, it doesnt tell why it hang, but instead timeout or ask to set a higher timeout.

---

I was wondering about async and found this answer. https://github.com/starship/starship/issues/301#issuecomment-529171336 starship architecture seem to be the problem.

Here is an additional tool that I have been using that integrates git with watchman, it surely makes repo faster after the first time you type `git status`.

https://github.com/jgavris/rs-git-fsmonitor

After setup, if works, you should see a message the first time you type `git status` in a repo.  
`Adding /path/project to Watchman's watch list`
@dougg0k `core.fsmonitor` values besides `true`, which uses the built-in git fsmonitor, are ignored by starship to avoid security issues in untrusted git repos.
I see. But `true` are not available on linux.

I'm not sure starship are ignoring anything, even with the tool, nothing changed in the current repos, information wise, yet it's faster when monitored / watched (<10ms).

What would be the indication that starship are ignoring a folder monitored, only the timeout?

---

I noticed that the timeout tends to happen right after a clone and on the cd to_folder, then never again, even with a command_timeout lower like 50. Also without being on watchman list. 

---

Regardless if it's because the new index are set in due to git config, which can improve speed more after the first git status. 

After the folder being on watchman list, it's even faster.

Even if `ls` are somewhat slow on those big projects, everytime you run the command, if I run `git status` to add to watchman list, every `ls` after is faster too.

---

I also activated git metrics, which was the only one disabled. All good.

I dont think there is anything else to be done regarding this.

My repo's `.git` content is around 40MB, and rest of the repo is around 50MB. 

```sh
❯ starship timings

 Here are the timings of modules in your prompt (>=1ms or output):
 git_status  -  336ms  -   "[?] "
 nodejs      -   22ms  -   "via  v20.18.3 "
 directory   -    1ms  -   "REDACTED "
 kubernetes  -    1ms  -   "⎈ REDACTED"
 git_branch  -   <1ms  -   "on  main "
 gcloud      -   <1ms  -   "on ☁️  REDACTED"
 line_break  -   <1ms  -   "\n"
 time        -   <1ms  -   "REDACTED"
 character   -   <1ms  -   "❯ "
```

`git status` seems to be taking the majority of the time.  Above timings seems consistent with direct cli call timings. Tested on bash and zsh with starship disabled.
```sh
# bash
bash-5.2$ time git status
On branch main
Your branch is up to date with 'origin/main'.

Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)
	REDACTED

nothing added to commit but untracked files present (use "git add" to track)

real	0m0.337s
user	0m0.042s
sys	0m0.310s

# zsh
✗ time git status
On branch main
Your branch is up to date with 'origin/main'.

Untracked files:
  (use "git add <file>..." to include in what will be committed)
	REDACTED

nothing added to commit but untracked files present (use "git add" to track)
git status  0.04s user 0.25s system 97% cpu 0.299 total
```

I guess I'll disable `git_status` module for now.
There are plenty of recommendations in this thread, you can try them to see if helps before disabling.
Someone changed the implementation and has improved performance in the newest version 1.23

Same problem (Max OS / kitty / zsh):

```
❯ starship --version
starship 1.23.0
branch:
commit_hash:
build_time:2025-04-27 15:52:14 +00:00
build_env:rustc 1.86.0 (05f9846f8 2025-03-31) (Homebrew),
```

```
❯ starship timings                                

 Here are the timings of modules in your prompt (>=1ms or output):
 git_status  -  293ms  -   ""
 java        -   18ms  -   "via ☕ v11.0.16 "
 directory   -    9ms  -   "my-large-repo "
 ruby        -    7ms  -   "via 💎 v3.3.5 "
 package     -    1ms  -   "is 📦 v1.x-SNAPSHOT "
 git_branch  -   <1ms  -   "on  integrate/25.12.1 "
 line_break  -   <1ms  -   "\n"
 character   -   <1ms  -   "❯ "
```

Tuning the git repo as described in https://www.git-tower.com/blog/git-performance/ didn't change anything.

Mentioning #5976 / #301 again as the "proper" solution

For the most part, you dont really tune the git repo, but your general `gitconfig`. Those are the ones that can make the most difference. Primarily the `fsmonitor` one. If not doing anything, try making sure you have `watchman` installed and are being used.

> For the most part, you dont really tune the git repo, but your general `gitconfig`. Those are the ones that can make the most difference. Primarily the `fsmonitor` one. If not doing anything, try making sure you have `watchman` installed and are being used.

Enabling `fsmonitor` will can also force starship to use the git-cli backend for git-status. If `core.fsmonitor` is `true`, the git index is sparse, or if `git_status.use_git_executable` is `true` starship will fall back to the git-cli instead of the gitoxide backend introduced in v1.23, which is usually faster.
At least here is what have made the most difference though. I didnt check without it after new update.

Powerlevel10k resolves this in a different way. It prints instantly and lets `git status` run in the background for longer, if it takes more than 10ms to display `git status`, then it prints the last known result, grayed out, and lets `git status` update the value async: https://github.com/romkatv/powerlevel10k/tree/36f3045d69d1ba402db09d09eb12b42eebe0fa3b?tab=readme-ov-file#why-does-git-status-sometimes-appear-grey-and-then-gets-colored-after-a-short-period-of-time

looks like it's not fixed yet.

```
starship timings

 Here are the timings of modules in your prompt (>=1ms or output):
 git_status  -  388ms  -   ""
 git_branch  -    2ms  -   "   master "
 directory   -    1ms  -   " ~/Co/v "
 character   -   <1ms  -   " ➜  "
 time        -   <1ms  -   " 󱑍 18:34 "
```

in my case, it's also `git_status` which takes majority of the share

```
❯ starship timings

 Here are the timings of modules in your prompt (>=1ms or output):
 sudo         -  20ms  -   ""
 git_metrics  -   3ms  -   "+3 "
 git_branch   -   2ms  -   "on  main "
 netns        -   1ms  -   ""
 directory    -  <1ms  -   "~/.dotfiles "
 git_status   -  <1ms  -   "[!] "
 line_break   -  <1ms  -   "\n"
 character    -  <1ms  -   "❯ "
 time         -  <1ms  -   "12:35:25 "
```

Tried in another project (bigger) without having used git status in the project to be added to watchman list.

```
❯ watchman watch-list
{
    "version": "20251222.073500.0",
    "roots": [
        "/mnt/NVME500GB/.dotfiles" // its a symlink
    ]
}

❯ starship timings

 Here are the timings of modules in your prompt (>=1ms or output):
 git_metrics  -  22ms  -   "+9708 -19800 "
 sudo         -  20ms  -   ""
 nodejs       -   4ms  -   "|  v24.12.0 "
 git_branch   -   2ms  -   "on  develop "
 netns        -   1ms  -   ""
 directory    -  <1ms  -   "/mnt/NVME500GB/projects/some_app "
 git_status   -  <1ms  -   "[✘!] "
 line_break   -  <1ms  -   "\n"
 character    -  <1ms  -   "❯ "
 time         -  <1ms  -   "12:36:36 "
```

---

Just tried with [kubernetes](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes) repo. Edited a file to show git status.

```
❯ git clone git@github.com:kubernetes/kubernetes.git
Cloning into 'kubernetes'...
remote: Enumerating objects: 2597127, done.
remote: Counting objects: 100% (1483/1483), done.
remote: Compressing objects: 100% (1021/1021), done.
remote: Total 2597127 (delta 704), reused 462 (delta 462), pack-reused 2595644 (from 2)
Receiving objects: 100% (2597127/2597127), 2.05 GiB | 16.13 MiB/s, done.
Resolving deltas: 100% (1872524/1872524), done.
Updating files: 100% (28205/28205), done.

❯ watchman watch-list
{
    "version": "20251222.073500.0",
    "roots": []
}

❯ starship timings

 Here are the timings of modules in your prompt (>=1ms or output):
 git_metrics  -  95ms  -   "+6 -5 "
 sudo         -  20ms  -   ""
 golang       -   4ms  -   "|  v1.25.5 "
 git_branch   -   2ms  -   "on  master "
 netns        -   1ms  -   ""
 directory    -  <1ms  -   "~/kubernetes "
 git_status   -  <1ms  -   "[!] "
 line_break   -  <1ms  -   "\n"
 character    -  <1ms  -   "❯ "
 time         -  <1ms  -   "12:57:48 "
```

> Powerlevel10k resolves this in a different way. It prints instantly and lets git status run in the background for longer, if it takes more than 10ms to display git status, then it prints the last known result, grayed out, and lets git status update the value async: https://github.com/romkatv/powerlevel10k/tree/36f3045d69d1ba402db09d09eb12b42eebe0fa3b?tab=readme-ov-file#why-does-git-status-sometimes-appear-grey-and-then-gets-colored-after-a-short-period-of-time

That's IMHO the best solution, I'll stick to powerlevel10k until this kind of solution is implemented in starship as starship is just not usable for me as I work often with large repositories like the `nixpkgs` git repo. I tried the Catppuccin preset, and just typing any command like `ls` hangs for maybe half a second (and I have an SSD drive!) irrespective of how many times I type it, while powerlevel10k always directly gives me a usable shell following the approach described in the above comment.

> starship is just not usable for me as I work often with large repositories like the `nixpkgs` git repo. I tried the Catppuccin preset, and just typing any command like `ls` hangs for maybe half a second (and I have an SSD drive!) irrespective of how many times I type it

@tobiasBora Do you by chance have FSMonitor (`core.fsmonitor`) enabled in Git? Disabling that made Starship much faster for me, but I usually work in smaller repos.

@injust Seems like it is already disabled:
```
❯ git config get core.fsmonitor
❯ git fsmonitor--daemon status
fatal: fsmonitor--daemon not supported on this platform
```

> > starship is just not usable for me as I work often with large repositories like the `nixpkgs` git repo. I tried the Catppuccin preset, and just typing any command like `ls` hangs for maybe half a second (and I have an SSD drive!) irrespective of how many times I type it
> 
> [@tobiasBora](https://github.com/tobiasBora) Do you by chance have FSMonitor (`core.fsmonitor`) enabled in Git? Disabling that made Starship much faster for me, but I usually work in smaller repos.

I work on big Android(AOSP) repositories and with or without `fsmonitor`, it does not help.
The only correct solution here is to replicate what Powerlevel10k does, which is to asynchronously update the status at a later point, without blocking the shell.

> ❯  git fsmonitor--daemon status          
fatal: fsmonitor--daemon not supported on this platform

https://github.com/jgavris/rs-git-fsmonitor

> https://github.com/jgavris/rs-git-fsmonitor

This might be faster, but it doesn't affect Starship. As in, Starship behaves as if FSMonitor is not being used.

I tested, it has small diferences. And, repos paths are added to watchman.

`watchman watch-list`
Starship doesn't care unless `core.fsmonitor` is `true`.

That I didnt check, but if not. Perhaps ask them to support custom fsmonitors.

https://github.com/starship/starship/issues/6519#issuecomment-2665542958
